design_philosophy.core_loop: Punch enemies, collect letter combinations to summon robotic satellites with distinct attack patterns, manage coin currency for power-ups, progress through non-linear stage selection before final confrontation
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management (coins vs. health/power) balanced against combat execution and satellite durability; robot destruction forces tactical replanning
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Cyborg supersoldier with interchangeable modular companions, each with unique abilities; fist-based combat authority and weapon flexibility through satellite combinations
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Strongly emphasizes player agency through optional stage order and satellite variety—eight distinct robot combinations encourage experimentation. Coins-as-currency creates meaningful opportunity cost. Transformation mechanic (same letter combo twice) rewards pattern mastery. Robotic companion system treats allies as consumable resources rather than permanent power-ups, creating risk/reward tension.

difficulty.lives_system: Starts with 2 extra lives; additional lives purchasable for 2000 coins at designated platforms
difficulty.continues_system: Unlimited continues available upon game over
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: non-linear stage selection (Areas B-F playable in any order) allows difficulty scaling via stage choice. Coin economy creates meaningful difficulty variance—player can invest heavily in health/power or hoard for transformation. Robot selection and satellite maintenance directly impact survivability.

progression.upgrade_system: Coin-based currency system: 300 coins restores health, 100 coins increases attack power (vest color changes from green to brown), 2000 coins grants extra life. Eight distinct robotic satellites unlocked via Greek letter (α/β) combinations with scaling power through transformation mechanic (dual same-combo activation enables temporary fusion form).
progression.unlock_mechanism: Areas B-F accessible immediately after Area A tutorial; Area G (final stage) unlocks only after all six intermediate stages are completed. Robots unlocked by collecting specific letter sequences during normal play.
replayability_hooks: Eight distinct satellite combinations with unique attack patterns encourage multiple playthroughs to master each
Non-linear stage order enables different difficulty trajectories and strategic boss sequence planning
Coin economy optimization—players can experiment with different upgrade priorities across runs
Boss test mode (accessible via debug cheat code) allows isolated practice against specific encounters

world_building_techniques: Instruction manual exposition: protagonist Steve Hermann loses both arms, receives cybernetic replacements from L.O.R.D., becomes agent 'Shatterhand' to defeat Metal Command military renegades
Stage theming reflects antagonist infrastructure: factory (Area A), industrial/military installations (Areas B-G) suggesting hierarchical progression through enemy organization
Character design and visual motifs (cyborg aesthetic, military branding) reinforce 1980s action-movie tone and technological setting (year 2030)

boss_design.philosophy: Multi-phase encounters requiring satellite adaptation; bosses designed to punish monotonic attack patterns. Regional variants (Solbrain vs. Shatterhand) show different AI: some bosses jump off-screen and attempt landing attacks vs. fixed position patterns. Boss placements shifted between regions, suggesting flexibility in difficulty curve design.
notable_bosses: Intro boss (Area A): accessible via boss test mode for practice
Area B boss: received graphical update in Solbrain; original version in Shatterhand had transformation sequence to worm-like second form (removed in regional variant)
Area C boss: swapped between regional versions; submarine level boss in Shatterhand, carnival-themed boss in Solbrain
Area E boss: received graphical redesign between versions
Area G final boss: accessible after all intermediate stages; presumably designed as climactic encounter requiring synthesis of learned satellite strategies

faction_indicators: Metal Command: militaristic antagonist faction seeking world conquest through cyborg army
L.O.R.D. (Law and Order Regulatory Division): governmental/law enforcement entity providing cybernetic augmentation to protagonist
danger_signals: Military renegades operating with autonomous cyborg soldiers
Protagonist's forced augmentation to match enemy technology—implicit arms race escalation
Staged progression through enemy bases suggests hierarchical command structure requiring systematic dismantling
environmental_storytelling: Area A (factory): industrial production site—likely cyborg manufacturing facility
Submarine base (Area C in Shatterhand): militarized underwater installation
Futuristic tower with missile (Area G): final fortification suggesting ultimate weapon or command center
Progressive architectural complexity reflects rising stakes and enemy organization depth

sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor - Shatterhand (extensive gameplay mechanics, debug features, regional differences, unused content documentation)
Wikipedia - Shatterhand (plot summary, gameplay overview, developer credits, release dates, reception context)
